class_schedule.topic: Programs, Portfolios, and Projects: Understanding the Hierarchy
learning_objectives: Distinguish between projects, programs, and portfolios
Explain how portfolio management drives strategic value
Identify the roles of portfolio manager, program manager, and project manager
readings: PMI Standard for Program Management, Ch. 1-2
PMI Standard for Portfolio Management, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Projects deliver outputs. Programs deliver outcomes by coordinating related projects. Portfolios deliver strategic value by selecting and balancing the right programs and projects.
key_concepts: Project: temporary endeavor creating a unique product or service
Program: group of related projects managed coordinately for benefits not available individually
Portfolio: collection of projects, programs, and operations managed to achieve strategic objectives
Portfolio value maximization: selecting the mix of investments that delivers the most strategic value
Role differences: PM delivers scope, program manager delivers benefits, portfolio manager delivers strategy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping a healthcare organization's projects into programs and a portfolio with strategic objectives

review.value: A program manages interdependencies between projects and focuses on benefits realization; a large project is still about delivering a single scope
Balance across risk levels, strategic themes, time horizons, and resource demands to avoid concentration risk

readings: PMI Standard for Portfolio Management, Ch. 4-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Portfolio governance determines which projects get funded, continued, or terminated. It is the organization's most powerful strategic execution tool.
key_concepts: Portfolio governance body: composition, authority, meeting cadence, decision criteria
Gate reviews: stage-gate decisions for project advancement or termination
Portfolio balancing: strategic alignment, risk profile, resource capacity, timeline distribution
Optimization models: constrained optimization across budget, resources, and strategic value
Portfolio performance reporting: aggregate status, health indicators, strategic contribution metrics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a portfolio review with go/kill/hold decisions for 10 active projects
setup_requirements: Project status summary cards for 10 projects

review.value: Go (continue to next phase), Kill (terminate), Hold (pause and reassess later), Recycle (rework current phase)
Maximize strategic value subject to budget and resource constraints; requires trade-off analysis

learning_objectives: Describe the program management lifecycle phases
Manage program dependencies and interfaces between projects
Design program architecture for component projects
readings: PMI Standard for Program Management, Ch. 3-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Program management coordinates related projects to deliver benefits greater than the sum of individual project deliverables.
key_concepts: Program lifecycle: definition, delivery, closure
Program architecture: how component projects relate and deliver collective benefits
Dependency management: identifying and tracking dependencies between program components
Program roadmap: timeline showing component projects, milestones, and benefit delivery points
Interface management: coordinating handoffs and shared resources between component projects
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a program roadmap for a smart city initiative with 5 component projects

review.value: Defines the structure of component projects, their relationships, and how they collectively deliver program benefits
Mandatory (hard), discretionary (soft), internal (between components), external (outside the program)

class_schedule.topic: Benefits Management Across Programs and Portfolios
learning_objectives: Design a benefits management framework for programs
Track benefits realization across the program lifecycle
Handle benefits that emerge after component project closure
readings: PMI Standard for Program Management, Ch. 6

lecture_notes.introduction: Benefits are the reason programs exist. Managing benefits requires a different mindset than managing project deliverables.
key_concepts: Benefits identification: mapping program outcomes to measurable benefits
Benefits register: inventory of expected benefits with owners, metrics, and target dates
Benefits transition: handing off responsibility from program team to operational owners
Emergent benefits: benefits that become apparent only after initial delivery
Benefits sustainment: ensuring benefits continue after the program closes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a benefits register for an enterprise ERP program with 4 component projects

review.value: Programs deliver capabilities; operational teams realize benefits; the handoff must be explicitly planned
Without active management, benefits decay over time; assign ongoing ownership and measurement

learning_objectives: Implement enterprise resource planning across a portfolio
Resolve resource conflicts between competing projects
Forecast and plan for strategic resource capacity
readings: PMI Standard for Portfolio Management, Ch. 7

lecture_notes.introduction: Resource constraints are the primary binding constraint on portfolios. You can fund more projects than you can staff.
key_concepts: Enterprise resource pool: shared resources across all portfolio components
Demand vs. capacity: aggregating resource demand from all projects against available capacity
Resource conflict resolution: strategic priority, contractual commitments, critical path impact
Capacity planning: forecasting future resource needs based on portfolio pipeline
Skills gap analysis: identifying capability gaps and planning hiring or training
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an enterprise resource demand vs. capacity chart and resolving three resource conflicts

review.value: When demand exceeds capacity, projects must be deferred, descoped, or outsourced; adding more projects creates multitasking waste
When two projects need the same resource, the higher strategic priority project gets the resource

learning_objectives: Design a portfolio performance measurement system
Apply lessons learned at the portfolio level
readings: PMI Standard for Portfolio Management, Ch. 8

lecture_notes.introduction: The portfolio is dynamic. Market conditions, strategic priorities, and project performance change constantly, requiring continuous portfolio optimization.
key_concepts: Portfolio dashboard: aggregate project health, strategic coverage, resource utilization, financial performance
Portfolio rebalancing: adding, terminating, or re-prioritizing projects based on changed conditions
Strategic drift detection: identifying when the portfolio no longer aligns with current strategy
Portfolio-level lessons learned: patterns across projects that indicate systemic organizational issues
Continuous improvement: feeding portfolio insights into strategic planning and PM capability development
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a quarterly portfolio rebalancing exercise with changed market conditions

review.value: When strategy changes but the portfolio does not, the organization executes yesterday's strategy; regular alignment reviews prevent this
If multiple projects have the same problem (e.g., poor estimation), the fix is at the organizational level, not the project level

questions.question: A portfolio has a $5M annual budget. Five projects are proposed: P1 (strategic value: High, cost: $2M), P2 (High, $1.5M), P3 (Medium, $1M), P4 (Medium, $1.5M), P5 (Low, $500K). Which projects should be funded and why?
questions.answer: Fund P1 ($2M) + P2 ($1.5M) + P3 ($1M) = $4.5M, leaving $500K. P5 ($500K, Low value) fits the remaining budget but has low strategic value. P4 ($1.5M, Medium) does not fit. Decision: Fund P1, P2, P3, and defer P4 and P5. The remaining $500K should be reserved for contingency or emerging opportunities rather than spending it on a low-value project. Strategic alignment takes priority over spending the entire budget.

rubric.key: Justifies not spending remaining budget on low-value project

questions.question: Explain how a program differs from a collection of related projects without a program structure. What specific value does the program management layer add?
questions.answer: Without a program structure, related projects are managed independently. Each PM focuses on their project's scope, schedule, and cost. Dependencies are managed ad hoc, benefits are tracked per project, and no one is responsible for the collective outcome. A program management layer adds: (1) coordinated dependency management preventing cross-project delays, (2) benefits management ensuring collective outcomes exceed individual project outputs, (3) shared resource optimization reducing conflicts, (4) architectural consistency across component deliverables, (5) a single point of accountability for the combined strategic objective. The value is integration and benefits that no single project can deliver alone.

rubric.key: Describes the problem without program structure
Identifies at least 3 specific values the program layer adds
Emphasizes benefits as the key differentiator

questions.question: A program has delivered all 4 component projects on time. The benefits register shows a target of 25% reduction in customer complaint resolution time. Six months post-delivery, the actual reduction is only 8%. What should the program manager do?
questions.answer: The program manager should: (1) Investigate the benefits gap by analyzing why the 25% target is not being met despite on-time delivery (likely an adoption or operational change issue), (2) Assess whether operational teams have been trained and are using the new capabilities, (3) Check if the benefits transition plan was executed and operational owners are actively managing benefits, (4) Identify specific barriers (user resistance, process gaps, missing integrations) preventing full benefit realization, (5) Develop a remediation plan with specific actions, owners, and timelines to close the gap, (6) Report to the governance body with findings and recommendations. The key insight is that project delivery success does not equal benefits realization.

rubric.key: Investigates root cause of benefits gap

questions.question: Two projects both need the same senior architect for 3 months. Project Alpha is strategically critical with a hard deadline. Project Beta has higher NPV but a flexible deadline. How should the portfolio manager resolve this?
questions.answer: Allocate the senior architect to Project Alpha because: (1) Strategic criticality with a hard deadline means any delay has immediate strategic consequences, (2) Beta's flexible deadline means deferring it by 3 months has manageable impact, (3) NPV alone should not override strategic priority when deadlines are hard constraints. However, the portfolio manager should also: (4) Assess whether Beta can use an alternative architect or external consultant for the overlap period, (5) Communicate the decision and rationale to both project teams, (6) Adjust Beta's timeline and update the portfolio schedule, (7) Flag this as a capacity issue for future resource planning.

rubric.key: Allocates to Alpha with strategic justification
Explains why NPV doesn't override strategic priority

major_assignments.title: Program and Portfolio Management Framework Design
major_assignments.description: Design a complete program and portfolio management framework for an organization, including portfolio governance, program architecture, benefits management, and resource optimization.
objectives: Design a portfolio governance framework with selection and review processes
Create a program architecture for a multi-project initiative
Develop a benefits management framework with tracking mechanisms
Build an enterprise resource management approach
deliverables: Program architecture diagram and dependency matrix for a 4-project program
Benefits management framework with register and transition plan (3-4 pages)
Enterprise resource demand vs. capacity analysis with conflict resolution process

criteria: Governance body composition and authority clearly defined
Selection criteria and scoring model are practical
Review cadence and decision processes documented
Dependencies between components identified and managed
Program roadmap shows realistic timeline and milestones
Benefits register is complete with metrics and owners
Measurement approach is realistic and sustainable
Demand vs. capacity analysis identifies conflicts
Conflict resolution criteria are strategic and objective
Capacity planning looks forward at least 12 months

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on portfolio governance, program management, and benefits realization

framework_project.description: Program and portfolio management framework design assignment

midterm_examination.description: Portfolio governance, project selection, and program architecture

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all program and portfolio management topics

class_participation.description: Participation in portfolio review simulations, case studies, and group exercises

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Portfolio review simulations require prepared participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: Portfolio review exercises may be done in small groups. The framework design assignment is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Properly cite all standards and frameworks referenced.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Spreadsheet application for portfolio analysis and resource planning. Presentation or diagramming software for program architecture.
